questions.prompt: What is a virtual machine (VM)?

choices.text: A software-created abstraction that gives each guest the illusion of its own private hardware
choices.explanation: A VM virtualizes the underlying hardware so each guest OS believes it has its own dedicated machine.

choices.text: A single process running a normal application
choices.explanation: A normal application process is not a virtualized machine; it lacks its own virtual hardware abstraction.

choices.text: A region of memory shared between two processes
choices.explanation: Shared memory between processes is an IPC mechanism, not a VM.
A ready-thread queue is a scheduling structure.

questions.summary: A VM is a software abstraction giving each guest the illusion of its own private hardware.

questions.prompt: What is a hypervisor (virtual machine monitor)?

choices.text: The software layer that creates and manages VMs, allocating real hardware among guests
choices.explanation: The hypervisor (VMM) is the control layer that creates VMs and shares the physical hardware among multiple guests.

choices.text: The bootstrap code stored in the boot block
choices.explanation: Bootstrap code in the boot block loads an OS, not VMs.

choices.text: A device driver for a printer
choices.explanation: A printer driver handles one device, not VM management.

choices.text: The CPU scheduler inside a single guest
choices.explanation: A guest's internal scheduler runs inside a VM; it is not the hypervisor.

questions.summary: A hypervisor is the layer that creates and manages VMs, sharing real hardware among guests.

questions.prompt: A Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor:

choices.text: Runs directly on the hardware with no host OS beneath it
choices.explanation: A Type 1 hypervisor sits directly on the hardware, managing guests without an intervening host OS (e.g., ESXi, Xen).

choices.text: Runs as an application on top of a host operating system
choices.explanation: Running as an app atop a host OS describes a Type 2 hypervisor.
A guest OS runs inside a VM, not as the hypervisor.
A device controller is I/O hardware.

questions.summary: A Type 1 hypervisor runs directly on the hardware with no host OS beneath it.

questions.prompt: A Type 2 (hosted) hypervisor:

choices.text: Runs as an application on top of an existing host operating system
choices.explanation: A Type 2 hypervisor runs as a normal application within a host OS (e.g., VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
Running directly on bare hardware is a Type 1 hypervisor.

choices.text: Is the firmware that boots the machine
choices.explanation: Boot firmware is unrelated to hosting VMs.
A free-space bitmap is file-system bookkeeping.

questions.summary: A Type 2 hypervisor runs as an application on top of an existing host OS.

questions.prompt: The key difference between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors is:

choices.text: Type 1 runs on bare metal (better performance/isolation); Type 2 runs atop a host OS (easier to install)
choices.explanation: Type 1 is bare-metal with stronger performance and isolation; Type 2 is convenient because it installs like an ordinary app on a host OS.

choices.text: Type 1 runs atop a host OS; Type 2 runs on bare metal
choices.explanation: This reverses the two types.

choices.text: Both require a guest OS to function as the hypervisor
choices.explanation: Neither uses a guest OS as the hypervisor itself.
They differ in where they run and their trade-offs.

questions.summary:
Type 1 runs on bare metal (better performance); Type 2 runs atop a host OS (easier install)."

### 2. Virtualization techniques (★★★)

questions.prompt: Trap-and-emulate virtualization works by:

choices.text: Running guest code natively, but trapping privileged instructions so the hypervisor emulates them
choices.explanation: In trap-and-emulate, unprivileged guest code runs directly; privileged operations trap to the hypervisor, which emulates their effect safely.

choices.text: Translating every guest instruction in software before execution
choices.explanation: Translating every instruction in software is binary translation.

choices.text: Modifying the guest OS source to call the hypervisor directly
choices.explanation: Modifying the guest to call the hypervisor is paravirtualization.

choices.text: Using a separate physical CPU per guest
choices.explanation: Dedicating a physical CPU per guest is not a virtualization technique but partitioning.

questions.summary: Trap-and-emulate runs guest code natively but traps privileged instructions for the hypervisor to emulate.

questions.prompt: Why was binary translation introduced for x86 virtualization?

choices.text: Some x86 privileged instructions did not trap cleanly, so the hypervisor rewrote them in software
choices.explanation: Classic x86 had "non-virtualizable" instructions that failed to trap properly, so binary translation rewrites problematic code on the fly.

choices.text: To modify guest OS source code
choices.explanation: Modifying guest source is paravirtualization, a different approach.

choices.text: To dedicate one CPU per guest
choices.explanation: One CPU per guest is partitioning, not translation.

choices.text: To eliminate the need for any hypervisor
choices.explanation: A hypervisor is still required to do the translation.

questions.summary: Binary translation rewrites non-trapping privileged x86 instructions in software so they can be virtualized.

questions.prompt: Paravirtualization differs from full virtualization in that:

choices.text: The guest OS is modified to be aware of the hypervisor and make explicit calls (hypercalls)
choices.explanation: Paravirtualization changes the guest to cooperate with the hypervisor via hypercalls, reducing overhead at the cost of needing a modified guest.
An unmodified guest is the goal of full virtualization, not paravirtualization.

choices.text: Every instruction is translated in software
choices.explanation: Translating every instruction is binary translation.

choices.text: No hypervisor is involved at all
choices.explanation: A hypervisor is still present to service hypercalls.

questions.summary: Paravirtualization modifies the guest to make explicit hypercalls to a cooperating hypervisor.

questions.prompt: Hardware-assisted virtualization (e.g., Intel VT-x, AMD-V) helps by:

choices.text: Adding CPU support (e.g., a new guest mode) so privileged guest operations trap efficiently without software tricks
choices.explanation: Hardware extensions add modes/structures so the CPU traps sensitive guest operations directly, making trap-and-emulate efficient without binary translation.

choices.text: Requiring the guest OS to be rewritten
choices.explanation: Rewriting the guest is paravirtualization, which hardware assistance avoids.
Software translation of every instruction is what hardware support replaces.

choices.text: Removing the need for memory at all
choices.explanation: Memory is still needed; this is unrelated.

questions.summary: Hardware-assisted virtualization adds CPU support so privileged guest operations trap efficiently without software tricks.

questions.prompt: Which orders the virtualization techniques by how much they avoid software overhead, roughly?

choices.text: Hardware-assisted (least overhead) → trap-and-emulate → binary translation; paravirtualization trades guest modification for speed
choices.explanation: Hardware assistance minimizes software intervention; pure binary translation is heaviest; paravirtualization is fast but needs a modified guest.
Binary translation is generally the heaviest, not fastest.
Paravirtualization specifically requires guest changes.
Trap-and-emulate runs most code natively; it does not translate every instruction.

questions.summary:
Hardware-assisted is lightest, binary translation heaviest, and paravirtualization trades guest changes for speed."

### 3. Memory & I/O virtualization (★★)

questions.prompt: Why must the hypervisor virtualize memory with an extra translation layer?

choices.text: Each guest thinks it owns physical memory from zero, so guest-physical addresses must map to real host-physical addresses
choices.explanation: Guests assume contiguous physical memory starting at 0, so the hypervisor adds a layer mapping guest-physical to host-physical (e.g., via nested/extended page tables).

choices.text: Because guests share one page table directly
choices.explanation: Guests must not share one page table directly; isolation requires separate mappings.

choices.text: Because memory does not need protection in VMs
choices.explanation: Memory protection is essential, not absent, in VMs.

choices.text: Because the guest has no virtual memory
choices.explanation: Guests still run their own virtual memory; the extra layer sits beneath it.

questions.summary: The hypervisor adds a layer mapping each guest's physical addresses to real host-physical memory.

questions.prompt: Nested (extended) page tables help by:

choices.text: Letting hardware translate guest-virtual → guest-physical → host-physical without trapping on every guest page-table change
choices.explanation: Nested page tables let the MMU do two-level translation in hardware, avoiding costly hypervisor traps on guest page-table updates.

choices.text: Eliminating the guest's own page tables
choices.explanation: The guest still maintains its own page tables; nesting adds a second level beneath.

choices.text: Removing the need for a hypervisor
choices.explanation: A hypervisor is still required.
Encryption is a separate confidentiality feature.

questions.summary: Nested page tables let hardware translate guest-virtual → guest-physical → host-physical without trapping each guest change.

questions.prompt: I/O virtualization must address the fact that:

choices.text: Multiple guests may want the same physical device, so the hypervisor multiplexes or emulates devices
choices.explanation: Since several guests share limited physical devices, the hypervisor emulates devices, multiplexes access, or assigns them directly (passthrough).
Devices can be shared via emulation/multiplexing.

choices.text: Guests directly own all hardware with no mediation
choices.explanation: Direct unmediated ownership by all guests would break isolation.
I/O is a central part of virtualization.

questions.summary:
I/O virtualization multiplexes or emulates physical devices so multiple guests can share them."

### 4. Containers vs VMs (★★★)

questions.prompt: How does a container differ from a virtual machine?

choices.text: A container shares the host kernel and isolates user space, while a VM runs a full guest OS on virtual hardware
choices.explanation: Containers virtualize at the OS level, sharing the host kernel and isolating only user space; VMs virtualize hardware and run separate guest kernels.

choices.text: A container runs its own kernel on virtual hardware; a VM shares the host kernel
choices.explanation: This reverses container and VM behavior.
They differ in isolation level and resource use.
A container is an isolated environment, not a hypervisor.

questions.summary: A container shares the host kernel and isolates user space; a VM runs a full guest OS on virtual hardware.

questions.prompt: A practical consequence of containers sharing the host kernel is:

choices.text: Containers are lighter and start faster than VMs but provide weaker isolation
choices.explanation: Sharing the kernel makes containers lightweight and quick to start, but the shared kernel is a larger common attack surface than a VM's separate kernel.

choices.text: Containers are always more isolated than VMs
choices.explanation: VMs generally provide stronger isolation than containers.

choices.text: Containers must each boot a full OS
choices.explanation: Containers do not boot a full OS; that's a VM trait.
Containers are a Linux-native technology (and run elsewhere too).

questions.summary: Sharing the host kernel makes containers lighter and faster than VMs but with weaker isolation.

questions.prompt: The Linux features that underpin containers are:

choices.text: Namespaces (isolating what a process sees) and cgroups (limiting resource usage)
choices.explanation: Namespaces give each container an isolated view (PIDs, mounts, network), and cgroups cap and account for CPU/memory/I/O usage.
Page tables/TLBs are memory-management hardware structures.
Semaphores/monitors are synchronization tools.
Boot block/superblock are file-system structures.

questions.summary:
Containers are built on namespaces (isolation) and cgroups (resource limits)."

### 5. Benefits & uses of VMs (★★)

questions.prompt: A core benefit of VMs for system administration is:

choices.text: Strong isolation between guests, so a crash or compromise in one VM doesn't affect others
choices.explanation: VMs isolate guests, containing faults and breaches within a single VM.

choices.text: Guaranteed zero overhead compared to bare metal
choices.explanation: Virtualization adds some overhead; it is not zero.

choices.text: Elimination of the need for an operating system
choices.explanation: Guests still run operating systems.
Seek time is a physical disk property, not changed by virtualization.

questions.summary: VMs provide strong isolation so a crash or breach in one guest doesn't affect others.

questions.prompt: VMs enable server consolidation by:

choices.text: Running many guest servers on one physical host, improving hardware utilization
choices.explanation: Consolidation packs multiple virtual servers onto fewer physical machines, raising utilization and cutting cost.

choices.text: Requiring one physical machine per service
choices.explanation: One machine per service is exactly what consolidation avoids.

choices.text: Removing the need for any hardware
choices.explanation: Hardware is still required to host the VMs.
The hypervisor is what makes consolidation possible.

questions.summary: VMs enable server consolidation by running many guests on one host, improving utilization.

questions.prompt: Live migration of a VM means:

choices.text: Moving a running VM from one physical host to another with minimal downtime
choices.explanation: Live migration relocates an active VM (its memory/state) to another host while it keeps running, easing maintenance and load balancing.
Deleting a VM is not migration.

choices.text: Converting a VM into a container
choices.explanation: Converting to a container is a different operation.
Rebooting firmware is unrelated.

questions.summary: Live migration moves a running VM to another host with minimal downtime.

questions.prompt: Snapshots of a VM are useful because:

choices.text: They capture a VM's state at a point in time, allowing rollback after changes or failures
choices.explanation: A snapshot records the VM's memory/disk state so you can revert to it (e.g., before a risky upgrade).
Snapshots preserve state; they don't erase the VM.

choices.text: They speed up the CPU clock
choices.explanation: They don't change CPU clock speed.
The guest OS remains part of the snapshot.

questions.summary:
Snapshots capture a VM's state for rollback after changes or failures."

### 6. Overhead & performance (★★)

questions.prompt: A source of virtualization overhead is:

choices.text: Traps and emulation of privileged operations and the extra memory-translation layer
choices.explanation: Trapping privileged instructions, emulating devices, and the additional guest→host memory translation all add overhead.
Virtualization does not run faster than bare metal in general.
I/O is virtualized, not removed.
Context switches still occur (and may increase).

questions.summary: Virtualization overhead comes from trapping/emulating privileged ops and the extra memory-translation layer.

questions.prompt: Why does hardware assistance reduce virtualization overhead compared to binary translation?

choices.text: The CPU traps sensitive operations directly, so the hypervisor needn't rewrite guest code at runtime
choices.explanation: With hardware support, sensitive instructions trap natively, eliminating the runtime code-rewriting cost of binary translation.

choices.text: It rewrites more instructions than binary translation
choices.explanation: Hardware assistance rewrites fewer (ideally no) instructions.

choices.text: It requires modifying the guest OS
choices.explanation: Modifying the guest is paravirtualization, not hardware assistance.
Memory protection is preserved, not removed.

questions.summary:
Hardware assistance traps sensitive ops directly, avoiding runtime code rewriting like binary translation needs."

### 7. Application-level virtualization (★)

questions.prompt: A language virtual machine like the JVM provides:

choices.text: A portable execution environment so the same bytecode runs on many platforms
choices.explanation: The JVM executes platform-independent bytecode, giving "write once, run anywhere" portability at the application level.

choices.text: Hardware-level virtualization of a whole physical machine
choices.explanation: Whole-machine hardware virtualization is what system hypervisors do, not the JVM.
Namespaces are an OS-level container feature.
A Type 1 hypervisor is bare-metal system virtualization, not a language VM.

questions.summary: A language VM like the JVM gives a portable environment so the same bytecode runs on many platforms.

questions.prompt: Emulation differs from virtualization in that:

choices.text: Emulation imitates a different instruction set in software (slow), while virtualization runs the native ISA mostly directly
choices.explanation: Emulation simulates a non-native architecture instruction by instruction (e.g., running ARM code on x86), which is slow; virtualization runs the same ISA largely on the real CPU.

choices.text: Emulation always runs faster than native
choices.explanation: Emulation is typically much slower than native.
Virtualization uses the native ISA, not a foreign one.
They differ in whether the target ISA matches the host.

questions.summary:
Emulation imitates a foreign instruction set in software (slow); virtualization runs the native ISA mostly directly."

### 8. Isolation & security (★)

questions.prompt: Why is VM isolation generally stronger than container isolation?

choices.text: Each VM has its own kernel, so the attack surface shared with others is smaller than containers' shared host kernel
choices.explanation: Separate guest kernels mean a kernel-level exploit in one VM doesn't directly expose others; containers share the host kernel, a broader common surface.

choices.text: Containers each run a separate kernel
choices.explanation: Containers share the host kernel; they do not each run a separate one.

choices.text: VMs share one kernel among all guests
choices.explanation: VMs do not share one kernel; that's the container model.
Containers do have isolated user space.

questions.summary: VMs isolate more strongly because each has its own kernel, unlike containers' shared host kernel.

questions.prompt: A VM escape is:

choices.text: An exploit where code in a guest breaks out to affect the hypervisor or other VMs
choices.explanation: A VM escape breaches the isolation boundary, letting guest code reach the hypervisor/host or sibling VMs — a serious security failure.

choices.text: A normal shutdown of a guest
choices.explanation: A normal shutdown is routine, not an escape.
Live migration is a management operation, not a breach.
A snapshot rollback restores state; it is not an escape.

questions.summary:
A VM escape is an exploit where guest code breaks isolation to affect the hypervisor or other VMs."

### 9. Cloud & scaling (★)

questions.prompt: Virtualization underpins cloud computing because:

choices.text: It lets providers carve physical servers into many isolated, on-demand virtual machines for different tenants
choices.explanation: Cloud providers use virtualization to partition hardware into isolated VMs allocated on demand to many tenants.

choices.text: It requires each tenant to own physical hardware
choices.explanation: Tenants don't own the physical hardware; that's the point of the cloud.

choices.text: It eliminates the need for hypervisors
choices.explanation: Hypervisors are essential to cloud virtualization.
Virtualization enables, not prevents, controlled resource sharing.

questions.summary: Virtualization lets cloud providers split servers into isolated, on-demand VMs for many tenants.

questions.prompt: Combining containers and VMs (e.g., containers inside VMs) is common because:

choices.text: It pairs container agility with VM-level isolation for multi-tenant safety
choices.explanation: Running containers within VMs gives fast, lightweight deployment plus the stronger isolation boundary of a VM between tenants.

choices.text: It removes the need for namespaces
choices.explanation: Namespaces are still used by the containers.

choices.text: It makes containers boot a full OS
choices.explanation: Containers still don't boot a full OS even inside a VM.
The kernel is not eliminated; the VM provides one for its containers.

questions.summary:
Containers-in-VMs pair container agility with VM-level isolation for multi-tenant safety."

### 10. Summary/compare (★)

questions.prompt: Which best summarizes the abstraction level of each technology?

choices.text: Hardware-level: hypervisor/VM; OS-level: container; application-level: language VM (e.g., JVM)
choices.explanation: They differ by layer: hypervisors virtualize hardware, containers virtualize the OS (user space), language VMs virtualize at the application/bytecode level.

choices.text: Container is hardware-level; hypervisor is application-level
choices.explanation: This swaps the levels of containers and hypervisors.

choices.text: Language VM virtualizes hardware; containers virtualize CPUs
choices.explanation: Language VMs don't virtualize hardware; containers don't virtualize CPUs in that sense.

choices.text: All three virtualize at the same level
choices.explanation: They clearly operate at different levels.

questions.summary: Hypervisors virtualize hardware, containers virtualize the OS, and language VMs virtualize at the application level.

questions.prompt: The fundamental trade-off between VMs and containers is:

choices.text: VMs give stronger isolation at higher overhead; containers give lower overhead at weaker isolation
choices.explanation: The central trade-off is isolation strength versus resource overhead: VMs isolate more but cost more; containers are lean but share the kernel.

choices.text: VMs are always lighter than containers
choices.explanation: Containers, not VMs, are lighter.

choices.text: Containers always isolate better than VMs
choices.explanation: VMs generally isolate better than containers.

choices.text: They have identical overhead and isolation
choices.explanation: Their overhead and isolation clearly differ.

questions.summary:
VMs trade higher overhead for stronger isolation; containers trade weaker isolation for lower overhead."

questions.prompt: What is a distributed system?

choices.text: A collection of independent computers that appear to users as a single coherent system
choices.explanation: A distributed system links separate machines so that, ideally, users experience them as one unified system.

choices.text: A single computer running many threads
choices.explanation: Many threads on one machine is multithreading, not a distributed system.

choices.text: A region of shared memory between two processes
choices.explanation: Shared memory between processes is local IPC.

choices.text: A queue of ready processes on one CPU
choices.explanation: A ready queue is a single-machine scheduling structure.

questions.summary: A distributed system is independent computers that appear to users as one coherent system.

questions.prompt: What does "transparency" mean as a distributed-system goal?

choices.text: Hiding the distribution so users/programs need not know where resources physically reside
choices.explanation: Transparency means the distributed nature (location, replication, migration) is hidden, so the system looks local and unified.

choices.text: Making all network traffic visible to users
choices.explanation: Making traffic visible is the opposite of transparency.
Encryption is a security mechanism, not transparency.

choices.text: Showing the physical location of every file
choices.explanation: Exposing physical locations defeats location transparency.

questions.summary: Transparency hides distribution so users needn't know where resources physically reside.

questions.prompt: What does "scalability" refer to in distributed systems?

choices.text: The ability to handle growth (more users, data, or nodes) without unacceptable performance loss
choices.explanation: Scalability is graceful handling of increasing load or size by adding resources.

choices.text: The ability to hide resource locations
choices.explanation: Hiding resource locations is transparency.

choices.text: The ability to keep secrets from attackers
choices.explanation: Keeping secrets is confidentiality (security).

choices.text: The ability to run on a single CPU
choices.explanation: Running on a single CPU is not a distributed-system goal.

questions.summary: Scalability is handling growth in users, data, or nodes without unacceptable performance loss.

questions.prompt: "Fault tolerance" in a distributed system means:

choices.text: The system continues operating (perhaps degraded) despite some component failures
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance lets the system keep working when nodes or links fail, often via replication and redundancy.
Hiding locations is transparency.
Encryption is a security feature, not fault tolerance.

choices.text: The system runs faster than a single machine
choices.explanation: Raw speed is not the definition of fault tolerance.

questions.summary: Fault tolerance keeps the system operating despite some component failures.

questions.prompt: A benefit of resource sharing in distributed systems is:

choices.text: Users on different machines can use remote resources (files, printers, compute) as if local
choices.explanation: Resource sharing lets nodes access each other's files, devices, and compute, improving utilization and capability.

choices.text: Each machine must duplicate every resource
choices.explanation: Duplicating every resource everywhere defeats sharing.

choices.text: No machine can access another's resources
choices.explanation: Forbidding cross-access is the opposite of sharing.

choices.text: Resources must all live on one server
choices.explanation: Centralizing all resources on one server isn't required for sharing.

questions.summary:
Resource sharing lets users on different machines use remote resources as if local."

### 2. Network protocol stack (★★★)

questions.prompt: What is a protocol stack (layered network model)?

choices.text: A set of layers, each providing services to the layer above and using the layer below
choices.explanation: Layering (e.g., OSI/TCP-IP) decomposes networking into layers, each offering a service to the one above and relying on the one below.

choices.text: A single monolithic block handling all networking at once
choices.explanation: A monolithic all-in-one block is the opposite of a layered stack.

choices.text: A queue of packets waiting for the CPU
choices.explanation: A CPU packet queue is an implementation detail, not the model.

choices.text: A free-space bitmap for the network
choices.explanation: A free-space bitmap is file-system bookkeeping.

questions.summary: A protocol stack is layers, each serving the layer above and using the one below.

questions.prompt: What does the IP (network) layer provide?

choices.text: Logical addressing and routing of packets between hosts across networks
choices.explanation: IP handles host addressing and routing, forwarding packets toward their destination across interconnected networks.

choices.text: Reliable, ordered byte streams between processes
choices.explanation: Reliable ordered byte streams are provided by TCP (transport layer).

choices.text: Physical transmission of bits on a wire
choices.explanation: Physical bit transmission is the link/physical layer.
Application message formats are the application layer.

questions.summary: The IP layer provides logical addressing and routing of packets between hosts.

questions.prompt: How does TCP differ from UDP?

choices.text: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable (ordered, acknowledged); UDP is connectionless and best-effort (no guarantees)
choices.explanation: TCP sets up a connection and guarantees ordered, acknowledged delivery; UDP just sends datagrams with no delivery/order guarantees.

choices.text: TCP is connectionless; UDP is reliable and ordered
choices.explanation: This reverses TCP and UDP.
UDP does not guarantee reliability or ordering.
Both carry application data; they differ in guarantees.

questions.summary: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and best-effort.

questions.prompt: When is UDP preferable to TCP?

choices.text: When low latency matters and occasional loss is acceptable (e.g., live video/voice, gaming)
choices.explanation: UDP's lack of handshake/retransmission gives lower latency, suiting real-time media where late data is useless anyway.

choices.text: When every byte must arrive in order without loss
choices.explanation: Guaranteed in-order, lossless delivery calls for TCP.

choices.text: When a reliable file transfer is required
choices.explanation: Reliable file transfer needs TCP's guarantees.

choices.text: When a persistent connection with acknowledgments is needed
choices.explanation: Persistent acknowledged connections are TCP's domain.

questions.summary: UDP suits low-latency, loss-tolerant uses like live video, voice, and gaming.

questions.prompt: The purpose of layering the protocol stack is:

choices.text: Modularity — each layer can be designed/changed independently as long as interfaces stay fixed
choices.explanation: Layering isolates concerns so a layer's internals can change without affecting others, as long as the interface contract holds.

choices.text: To make all networking run in one inseparable unit
choices.explanation: An inseparable unit is the opposite of layering.

choices.text: To eliminate the need for IP addresses
choices.explanation: IP addressing is still needed at the network layer.
The transport layer remains essential.

questions.summary:
Layering gives modularity: each layer can change independently behind fixed interfaces."

### 3. Naming & addressing (★★)

questions.prompt: What does DNS (Domain Name System) do?

choices.text: Translates human-readable names (e.g., example.com) into IP addresses
choices.explanation: DNS maps domain names to IP addresses so users use names while the network uses addresses.
Routing packets is the IP layer's job.
Reliable byte streams come from TCP.
Encrypting web traffic is TLS, not DNS.

questions.summary: DNS translates human-readable names into IP addresses.

questions.prompt: A port number identifies:

choices.text: Which process/service on a host should receive the data
choices.explanation: While the IP address selects the host, the port selects the specific service/process within that host.

choices.text: Which physical machine on the network
choices.explanation: Selecting the machine is the IP address's role.
Free memory is unrelated to ports.

choices.text: The disk block of a file
choices.explanation: Disk blocks are storage details.

questions.summary:
A port number identifies which process/service on a host should receive the data."

### 4. Communication models (★★)

questions.prompt: A socket is:

choices.text: An endpoint for communication, identified by an IP address and port, used by processes to send/receive data
choices.explanation: A socket is the programming endpoint (IP + port) through which processes exchange network data.
A free disk block is storage bookkeeping.
A page table entry is a memory-management structure.
A CPU register is hardware state.

questions.summary: A socket is a communication endpoint identified by IP address and port.

questions.prompt: Remote Procedure Call (RPC) lets a program:

choices.text: Call a procedure that executes on a remote machine as if it were a local call
choices.explanation: RPC abstracts network communication so a remote function call looks like a local one, with stubs marshalling arguments.

choices.text: Share memory directly with a remote process
choices.explanation: Direct memory sharing across machines isn't possible the way local shared memory is; RPC uses messages.
Routing packets is the IP layer's job.
Name-to-address translation is DNS.

questions.summary: RPC lets a program call a procedure on a remote machine as if it were local.

questions.prompt: In RPC, marshalling (serialization) is:

choices.text: Packing procedure arguments into a message format suitable for network transmission
choices.explanation: Marshalling converts arguments/data into a transmittable byte format (and unmarshalling reverses it at the receiver).
Routing is the network layer's role, not marshalling.
Disk encryption is unrelated.
CPU scheduling is unrelated.

questions.summary:
Marshalling packs procedure arguments into a transmittable message format."

### 5. Replication & consistency (★★★)

questions.prompt: Why do distributed systems replicate data?

choices.text: To improve availability and performance — copies survive failures and serve nearby reads
choices.explanation: Replication keeps multiple copies so the system tolerates failures and serves reads from closer/less-loaded replicas.

choices.text: To guarantee there is exactly one copy of everything
choices.explanation: A single copy is the opposite of replication.

choices.text: To eliminate the need for a network
choices.explanation: A network is still needed to coordinate replicas.
Replication supports sharing, not prevents it.

questions.summary: Replication improves availability and performance because copies survive failures and serve nearby reads.

questions.prompt: What does the CAP theorem state?

choices.text: In the presence of a network partition, a system must choose between consistency and availability
choices.explanation: CAP says that during a partition you cannot have both full consistency and full availability; you must trade one off.

choices.text: A system can always have consistency, availability, and partition tolerance simultaneously
choices.explanation: You cannot fully guarantee all three at once when partitions happen.

choices.text: Consistency and availability are the same property
choices.explanation: Consistency (same view) and availability (always responds) are distinct.

choices.text: Partitions never occur in real networks
choices.explanation: Partitions do occur in real networks, which is why CAP matters.

questions.summary: CAP: under a network partition, a system must choose between consistency and availability.

questions.prompt: "Strong consistency" means:

choices.text: After an update, all replicas reflect the new value before any read can return the old one
choices.explanation: Strong consistency ensures every read sees the latest write, as if there were a single up-to-date copy.

choices.text: Replicas may return stale values for a while, converging later
choices.explanation: Temporary staleness that converges is eventual consistency.
Strong consistency does not forbid replication; it coordinates replicas.
Ignoring updates is not consistency at all.

questions.summary: Strong consistency means every read sees the latest write across all replicas.

questions.prompt: "Eventual consistency" means:

choices.text: Replicas may temporarily diverge but converge to the same value if no new updates occur
choices.explanation: Eventual consistency allows temporary divergence with a guarantee that replicas converge once updates stop.

choices.text: All replicas are always identical at every instant
choices.explanation: Always-identical-at-every-instant is strong consistency.
Updates are propagated; they just take time.

choices.text: Reads always block until global agreement
choices.explanation: Blocking until global agreement describes a stronger model.

questions.summary: Eventual consistency lets replicas diverge briefly but converge once updates cease.

questions.prompt: A quorum-based approach maintains consistency by:

choices.text: Requiring reads/writes to involve a majority (quorum) of replicas so they overlap and detect the latest value
choices.explanation: Overlapping read/write quorums (e.g., majorities) guarantee that any read intersects the latest write, preserving consistency.

choices.text: Contacting only one replica for every operation
choices.explanation: Contacting one replica risks reading stale data.
No coordination would break consistency.
Page replacement is a virtual-memory concept.

questions.summary:
Quorums require a majority of replicas for reads/writes so they overlap and reveal the latest value."

### 6. Distributed coordination (★★)

questions.prompt: Why are physical clocks insufficient for ordering events across machines?

choices.text: Clocks drift and cannot be perfectly synchronized, so timestamps may disagree about order
choices.explanation: Independent clocks drift and synchronization has error, so raw timestamps can misorder events — motivating logical clocks.

choices.text: All machines share one perfectly synchronized clock
choices.explanation: Machines do not share one perfect clock.
Ordering events is often essential (e.g., causality).
Real networks have nonzero, variable latency.

questions.summary: Physical clocks drift and can't be perfectly synced, so timestamps may disagree about event order.

questions.prompt: A logical clock (e.g., Lamport timestamps) provides:

choices.text: A way to order events consistently based on causality rather than real time
choices.explanation: Logical clocks assign counters that respect causal "happens-before" ordering without relying on synchronized real time.
They don't provide synchronized wall-clock time.
Routing is the IP layer's job.
Name resolution is DNS.

questions.summary: A logical clock orders events by causality rather than real time.

questions.prompt: A distributed election algorithm is used to:

choices.text: Choose a coordinator/leader among nodes, e.g., after the current one fails
choices.explanation: Election algorithms (e.g., bully, ring) pick a new leader/coordinator when one is needed or the prior one fails.
Encryption is a security mechanism.
Routing is the network layer's job.
Page replacement is virtual memory.

questions.summary:
A distributed election algorithm chooses a coordinator/leader among nodes."

### 7. Distributed file systems (★★)

questions.prompt: A distributed file system (DFS) lets:

choices.text: Clients access files stored on remote servers through a unified namespace
choices.explanation: A DFS presents remote files under a common namespace so clients use them like local files.
Restricting to local files defeats the purpose of a DFS.
Files persist on storage, not only in memory.

choices.text: Each machine see a completely different set of files with no sharing
choices.explanation: A DFS enables sharing through a unified view, not isolation.

questions.summary: A DFS lets clients access remote files through a unified namespace.

questions.prompt: Caching in a DFS improves performance but requires:

choices.text: A cache-consistency protocol so clients don't act on stale copies
choices.explanation: Client/server caching speeds access but needs protocols to invalidate or update stale cached data.
Replication and caching often coexist; disabling replication isn't required.
The network is fundamental to a DFS.
A single global copy contradicts caching's purpose.

questions.summary:
DFS caching needs a consistency protocol so clients don't act on stale copies."

### 8. Failure handling (★★)

questions.prompt: Why is failure detection hard in distributed systems?

choices.text: A slow/unreachable node is indistinguishable from a crashed one over an unreliable network
choices.explanation: Without bounded delays, you can't tell a crashed node from a slow or partitioned one, complicating detection.

choices.text: Nodes always announce their own failures instantly
choices.explanation: Failed nodes can't reliably announce their own failure.

choices.text: Networks deliver every message immediately and reliably
choices.explanation: Real networks have delays and losses.
Failures do occur.

questions.summary: A slow or partitioned node looks the same as a crashed one over an unreliable network.

questions.prompt: Replication aids fault tolerance because:

choices.text: If one replica fails, others still hold the data and can serve requests
choices.explanation: Multiple copies mean a single replica's failure doesn't lose the data or stop service.

choices.text: It guarantees only one copy exists
choices.explanation: Replication means multiple copies, not one.

choices.text: It removes the need for consistency
choices.explanation: Replication actually raises consistency concerns, not removes them.
It cannot prevent partitions from occurring.

questions.summary:
Replication aids fault tolerance because surviving replicas still hold the data and serve requests."

### 9. Trade-offs (★)

questions.prompt: The fundamental tension captured by CAP during a partition is between:

choices.text: Consistency (all nodes see the same data) and availability (every request gets a response)
choices.explanation: When the network partitions, a system must sacrifice either consistency or availability — it cannot fully provide both.
Latency vs throughput is a general performance trade-off, not CAP's core.
Confidentiality vs integrity are security goals.
Seek time vs rotational delay are disk-performance factors.

questions.summary: During a partition, CAP forces a choice between consistency and availability.

questions.prompt: Choosing eventual over strong consistency typically buys:

choices.text: Higher availability and lower latency at the cost of possibly reading stale data
choices.explanation: Relaxing to eventual consistency lets nodes respond without global coordination, improving availability/latency but allowing temporary staleness.

choices.text: Guaranteed up-to-date reads at all times
choices.explanation: Guaranteed up-to-date reads is strong consistency, the thing being relaxed.
Eventual consistency still uses replication.
Confidentiality is a separate security concern.

questions.summary: Eventual consistency buys availability and low latency at the cost of possibly stale reads.

questions.prompt: Which statement best contrasts TCP/UDP with the consistency trade-off?

choices.text: TCP vs UDP trades reliability for latency; strong vs eventual consistency trades freshness guarantees for availability — both are reliability-versus-performance choices
choices.explanation: Both are reliability/performance trade-offs: TCP adds guarantees at latency cost like strong consistency adds freshness at availability cost.
Transport protocols and consistency models are different mechanisms, only analogous.
UDP is a transport protocol and does not provide data-consistency guarantees.
TCP does not remove the CAP theorem, which concerns distributed data.

questions.summary:
TCP/UDP and strong/eventual consistency are both reliability-versus-performance trade-offs at different layers."

## [Code]

questions.prompt:
Based on the socket pseudo-code, which protocol is being used and what guarantee does it give?
```c
s = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);   // (A)
connect(s, server_addr);               // handshake
send(s, data);                         // ordered, acknowledged
recv(s, buf);
```

choices.explanation: `SOCK_STREAM` plus an explicit `connect` handshake indicates TCP, which gives connection-oriented, reliable, ordered byte-stream delivery.
UDP uses `SOCK_DGRAM` and has no connect handshake or delivery guarantees.
DNS resolves names to addresses; it isn't a socket stream type.
Marshalling serializes RPC arguments; it isn't a transport protocol.

questions.summary:
SOCK_STREAM with a connect handshake is TCP, giving connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery."
